text:
BREAST MEDICAL ONCOLOGY
NEW PATIENT CONSULTATION — MULTIDISCIPLINARY TUMOR BOARD CASE

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: Breast Medical Oncology
CONSULTATION TYPE: New Patient — Post-MDT Treatment Planning
REFERRING SERVICE: Breast Surgical Oncology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Maria Elena Reyes is a 45-year-old premenopausal Hispanic woman who presents for new patient breast medical oncology consultation following multidisciplinary tumor board (MDT) review of her newly diagnosed left breast cancer. She was referred by her surgical oncologist after completion of initial diagnostic workup. She presents today with her husband to discuss treatment recommendations and proceed with neoadjuvant systemic therapy planning.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Reyes is a 45-year-old premenopausal woman who first noticed a palpable left breast lump approximately 6 weeks ago while performing self-examination. She describes the mass as firm and non-tender, located in the left upper outer breast. She did not notice any skin changes, nipple discharge, or arm swelling at that time. She presented to her primary care physician, who confirmed the palpable mass on exam and referred her urgently for imaging.

Diagnostic mammography and targeted ultrasound were performed, revealing a 3.2–3.4 cm irregular, spiculated mass in the left upper outer quadrant at approximately 10 o'clock, 4 cm from the nipple, with BI-RADS 5 assessment. Ultrasound of the left axilla identified one morphologically abnormal level I lymph node with cortical thickening and loss of fatty hilum, suspicious for nodal involvement. Right breast and axilla were unremarkable.

A core needle biopsy of the left breast mass and fine needle aspiration of the suspicious left axillary node were subsequently performed. Pathology confirmed invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3, Nottingham score 9/9. IHC panel demonstrated: ER negative (Allred 0), PR negative (Allred 0), HER2 IHC 1+ with FISH non-amplified (HER2/CEP17 ratio 1.2), confirming triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC). Ki-67 was 85%. PD-L1 by 22C3 antibody: Combined Positive Score (CPS) 18, which is positive (threshold ≥10). FNA of the axillary node was positive for metastatic carcinoma. Stromal TILs were approximately 40%. Lymphovascular invasion was identified in the biopsy cores.

Staging workup was completed. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast showed no evidence of distant metastatic disease. Left axillary adenopathy at 1.8 cm was noted, consistent with known nodal disease. Breast MRI (pre-treatment baseline) demonstrated the left breast primary measuring 3.5 x 2.9 x 2.7 cm with rapid initial enhancement and washout kinetics, and identified three morphologically abnormal level I left axillary nodes (largest 1.9 cm), slightly more than identified by ultrasound alone. No additional occult foci in either breast.

Comprehensive genomic profiling (FoundationOne CDx) was performed on the biopsy specimen and revealed a complex, high-risk genomic profile: TP53 p.R248W mutation (VAF 48%, pathogenic), PTEN homozygous deletion, MYC high-level amplification (copy number 18), RB1 loss-of-function mutation (p.R579*, VAF 42%), CDKN2A homozygous deletion, CDK6 amplification, and several additional mutations in chromatin remodeling genes (MLL3/KMT2C, ARID1A, SPEN, FAT1, NF1, NOTCH1). No BRCA1/2 somatic mutations detected. HRD score 42 (borderline, below ola eligibility threshold). TMB 8 mutations/Mb (intermediate). MSS. No actionable fusions. Germline testing was also sent and confirmed BRCA1 and BRCA2 wildtype status; PALB2 wildtype; ATM p.V2424G (variant of uncertain significance).

The case was reviewed at this week's multidisciplinary breast tumor board, attended by breast medical oncology, breast surgical oncology, radiation oncology, and pathology. The board's consensus recommendation is neoadjuvant systemic therapy, with the goal of tumor downstaging, limb-sparing surgery assessment, and evaluation of pathologic response — which carries important prognostic significance in TNBC. The patient is seen today to review these recommendations, discuss the treatment plan and its rationale, address her questions and concerns, and obtain informed consent.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY

1. No prior personal history of breast cancer, ovarian cancer, or other malignancy.
2. No prior breast biopsies or atypical histologic diagnoses.
3. Gravida 2, para 2. Two uncomplicated vaginal deliveries. No history of pregnancy complications.
4. No known cardiovascular disease. No hypertension, diabetes mellitus, or dyslipidemia.
5. No prior chemotherapy or radiation therapy.
6. Menstrual history: regular cycles, premenopausal at diagnosis.
7. No significant surgical history beyond obstetric deliveries.

---

FAMILY HISTORY

- Maternal aunt: breast cancer, diagnosed at age 52 (details of subtype unknown).
- No known family history of ovarian cancer, pancreatic cancer, prostate cancer, or melanoma at time of this visit.
- No known family history of BRCA mutation. No prior genetic testing in family members reported.
- Father: alive, no significant cancer history. Mother: alive, no personal cancer history.
- Two siblings: healthy, no known malignancies.

Note: Given germline BRCA1/2 negative status and maternal aunt with breast cancer, genetic counseling to be ongoing. Expanded panel (PALB2, CHEK2, ATM, etc.) was sent and results pending at time of consultation.

---

SOCIAL HISTORY

- Occupation: High school teacher (mathematics); currently working full-time.
- Born in Mexico; immigrated to the southwestern United States; Spanish-speaking with functional English.
- Married, living with husband and two children.
- Never-smoker. Occasional alcohol (1-2 drinks per week, social). No illicit drug use.
- BMI 26.4 kg/m². No significant dietary restrictions.
- Physically active: walks 3-4 days per week, occasional cycling.
- Support system: described as strong — husband and family actively involved. Patient is motivated and engaged.
- Health insurance: confirmed, authorizations being processed for upcoming therapies.
- Patient is fluent in Spanish; medical interpreter services offered and accepted for portions of today's visit.

---

ALLERGIES

- No known drug allergies (NKDA).
- No known food or latex allergies reported.

---

MEDICATIONS

- No current prescription medications.
- Prenatal/multivitamin: not currently taking.
- Ibuprofen: occasional use for headaches (OTC).
- No herbal supplements, traditional remedies, or alternative medicine agents currently reported.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Reports mild fatigue since the time of her diagnosis, which she attributes predominantly to stress and anxiety. No fever, chills, or drenching night sweats. Weight stable (no unintentional weight loss).

HEENT: No headaches, visual changes, or oral lesions.

Pulmonary: No dyspnea, cough, or hemoptysis.

Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or lower extremity edema.

Gastrointestinal: Normal appetite. No nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or constipation at baseline. No abdominal pain.

Musculoskeletal: No bone pain. No joint swelling or arthralgia.

Neurological: No headaches, numbness, tingling, or weakness. No cognitive changes.

Skin: No rashes or lesions noted.

Breast/Lymphatic: Palpable left breast mass, as described. No right breast changes. No arm swelling, lymphedema symptoms.

Psychiatric: Patient reports significant anxiety related to diagnosis. States she is "trying to stay strong" for her children but tearful at times. No current depression, no suicidal ideation. Interested in psychosocial support referral.

Endocrine: Menstrual cycles regular, premenopausal. No heat/cold intolerance, no polyuria/polydipsia.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Well-appearing Hispanic woman, alert and oriented, in no acute distress. Accompanied by husband. Tearful at moments during history-taking but engaged and asking detailed questions throughout.

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 118/72 mmHg, heart rate 76 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16/min, temperature 36.8°C, oxygen saturation 98% on room air, weight 68 kg, height 160 cm, BMI 26.6 kg/m².

ECOG Performance Status: 0.

HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Pupils equal and reactive. Oropharynx without erythema or exudate. No cervical lymphadenopathy.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, gallops, or rubs. No JVD.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatomegaly or splenomegaly. No palpable abdominal masses.

Breast Exam:
— Left breast: Firm, irregular, fixed mass palpable in the upper outer quadrant at approximately 10 o'clock, 4 cm from the nipple, estimated 3–3.5 cm in greatest dimension by palpation. Overlying skin intact. No skin thickening, erythema, or peau d'orange. No nipple retraction or discharge. The mass has indistinct borders and is mildly mobile relative to the chest wall.
— Right breast: No palpable masses, no skin changes, no nipple discharge.

Axillary Exam:
— Left axilla: One palpable, firm, round lymph node, approximately 1.5–2 cm, in level I position. Mildly mobile, non-tender. Consistent with known metastatic nodal disease on biopsy.
— Right axilla: No palpable lymphadenopathy.
— Bilateral supraclavicular and infraclavicular fossae: No palpable adenopathy.

Extremities: No lower extremity edema. No lymphedema of upper extremities.

Neurological: Alert and oriented x4. No focal neurological deficits.

Skin: No rashes. No spider angiomata or jaundice.

---

DIAGNOSTIC DATA

Laboratory Results (obtained at time of staging workup):
- CBC: WBC 6.8 x10⁹/L, Hgb 13.2 g/dL, Hct 39.5%, Plt 278 x10⁹/L. No cytopenias.
- BMP: Na 139 mEq/L, K 4.0 mEq/L, Cr 0.8 mg/dL, BUN 14 mg/dL, glucose 88 mg/dL.
- LFTs: AST 22 U/L, ALT 18 U/L, Alk Phos 68 U/L, total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL, albumin 4.1 g/dL. All within normal limits.
- Cardiac function: ECHO (baseline, obtained prior to initiating anthracycline): LVEF 63%, normal left ventricular systolic function, no wall motion abnormalities, no pericardial effusion.
- LDH 192 U/L (normal).
- TSH: 2.4 mIU/L (normal).
- Pregnancy test: negative (serum beta-hCG).
- CA 15-3 and CEA: not routinely obtained for staging in this setting per institutional guidelines.

Key Pathology Summary (reviewed in detail):
- Left breast core biopsy: IDC grade 3, Nottingham 9/9. ER 0%, PR 0%, HER2 IHC 1+, FISH non-amplified. Ki-67 85%. PD-L1 CPS 18 (positive). TILs ~40%. LVI present.
- Left axillary FNA: positive for metastatic carcinoma.
- TNBC subtype confirmed.

Key Imaging Summary:
- Mammogram/Ultrasound: 3.2–3.4 cm left upper outer mass; one abnormal left axillary node.
- CT chest/abdomen/pelvis: no distant metastatic disease; left axillary adenopathy 1.8 cm.
- Breast MRI (baseline): Primary 3.5 x 2.9 x 2.7 cm, washout kinetics; three abnormal left level I axillary nodes (largest 1.9 cm). Clinical stage: cT2N1M0.

NGS (FoundationOne CDx):
- TP53 p.R248W, PTEN deletion, MYC amplification, RB1 loss, CDKN2A deletion, CDK6 amplification, multiple additional alterations as noted above.
- No BRCA1/2 somatic mutations; no HER2 amplification; MSS; TMB 8 mut/Mb.
- Overall genomic profile consistent with high-risk, highly aggressive TNBC phenotype.

---

ASSESSMENT

1. Stage IIB (cT2N1M0) triple-negative invasive ductal carcinoma of the left breast, grade 3, Nottingham score 9/9, newly diagnosed in a 45-year-old premenopausal woman.
   - High-risk features: TNBC subtype, grade 3, Ki-67 85%, LVI, axillary nodal involvement (biopsy-proven), high-level MYC amplification, TP53 mutation, PTEN loss, RB1 loss.
   - Favorable immunologic features: TILs 40%, PD-L1 CPS 18 — supporting eligibility for and anticipated benefit from checkpoint immunotherapy in the neoadjuvant setting.
   - BRCA1/2 germline negative; HRD score 42 (borderline, below PARP inhibitor eligibility threshold per gBRCA-negative criteria).
   - No distant metastatic disease on staging workup (CT CAP, breast MRI).

2. ECOG performance status 0. Premenopausal. No significant comorbidities. Adequate organ function. Appropriate candidate for full-dose systemic therapy.

---

PLAN

The case was reviewed at the multidisciplinary breast tumor board. The following treatment plan was discussed in detail with the patient and her husband today during this visit, with the aid of a Spanish medical interpreter.

1. NEOADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY — KEYNOTE-522 REGIMEN

   Rationale: In stage II-III TNBC, neoadjuvant systemic therapy is the preferred approach. It allows assessment of in-vivo chemosensitivity, potential tumor downstaging to improve surgical options (including breast conservation), and provides critical prognostic information through pathologic response assessment. The KEYNOTE-522 trial demonstrated a significant improvement in pathologic complete response rate and event-free survival with the addition of Keytruda to neoadjuvant chemotherapy in early-stage TNBC, with benefit seen regardless of PD-L1 status — though our patient's CPS of 18 is robustly positive.

   Proposed regimen:
   - Phase 1 (AC phase): pembro 200 mg IV on day 1 every 3 weeks PLUS dose-dense doxo 60 mg/m² IV and Cytoxan 600 mg/m² IV every 2 weeks x 4 cycles.
     - Growth factor support: pegfilgrastim 6 mg subcutaneous injection on day 2 of each AC cycle (required for dose-dense scheduling).
     - Antiemetics: ondansetron, dexamethasone, lorazepam per protocol.
   - Phase 2 (taxane phase): Keytruda 200 mg IV every 3 weeks PLUS weekly Taxol 80 mg/m² IV x 12 doses (given weekly over 12 weeks).
   - Total neoadjuvant Keytruda: approximately 6 doses (2 during AC phase, 4 during pacli phase), then continued into adjuvant phase to complete approximately 1 year total.

   Pre-treatment echocardiogram reviewed — LVEF 63%, acceptable for anthracycline therapy. Baseline labs reviewed — organ function adequate.

2. SURGERY (planned after completion of neoadjuvant therapy):
   Surgical oncology will reassess for breast-conserving surgery vs. mastectomy following restaging MRI after neoadjuvant therapy. The patient has expressed interest in preserving her breast if oncologically appropriate. Sentinel lymph node biopsy and/or axillary lymph node dissection will be determined by surgical oncology based on response.

3. POST-NEOADJUVANT ADJUVANT THERAPY (contingent on pathologic response):
   - If pathologic complete response (pCR) achieved: adjuvant Keytruda to complete 1 year total.
   - If residual disease present (non-pCR): adjuvant cape (per CREATE-X trial data) plus completion of pembro to 1 year total will be discussed.

4. RADIATION ONCOLOGY: Radiation oncology consultation placed. Adjuvant radiation planning will proceed after surgery.

5. FERTILITY PRESERVATION COUNSELING:
   Patient was counseled extensively regarding the risk of chemotherapy-associated premature ovarian insufficiency (POI) and potential impact on fertility. She is premenopausal with two existing children. She was referred to reproductive endocrinology/fertility specialist and offered oocyte cryopreservation prior to initiation of chemotherapy. After thorough discussion with her husband, the patient declined oocyte cryopreservation, citing her family is complete and given the urgency of starting treatment. This decision was documented. Menstrual cycle monitoring and ovarian function testing (AMH, FSH) discussed as ongoing surveillance.

6. COUNSELING — TOXICITIES AND EXPECTATIONS:
   The patient received extensive counseling (with interpreter assistance) regarding expected adverse effects of this regimen, including:
   - Alopecia (expected to begin within 2–3 weeks of AC initiation; complete scalp hair loss anticipated). Patient visibly distressed by this; provided with referral to wig fitter and counseled on scalp cooling (determined not eligible due to known nodal disease and cancer type).
   - Nausea and vomiting: expected with AC; antiemetic regimen discussed.
   - Fatigue: anticipated throughout chemotherapy course; discussed energy conservation strategies and light physical activity.
   - Immunosuppression and infection risk: neutropenia expected; pegfilgrastim provided for mitigation. Counseled on signs of febrile neutropenia and when to seek emergency care (fever ≥38.3°C).
   - Peripheral neuropathy: risk with weekly Taxol, particularly cumulative; patient instructed to report early tingling/numbness.
   - Immune-related adverse events (irAEs) from Keytruda: discussed spectrum including immune-mediated colitis, pneumonitis, hepatitis, endocrinopathies (thyroid dysfunction, adrenal insufficiency), dermatitis, arthritis. Patient instructed to report any new or unusual symptoms promptly. Provided with immunotherapy patient education card.
   - Cardiac monitoring: given anthracycline exposure, repeat ECHO planned per institutional protocol.

7. PSYCHOSOCIAL SUPPORT:
   Patient expressed anxiety about her diagnosis and the impact on her family. Referral placed to oncology social work for psychosocial support. Navigation to Spanish-language breast cancer support groups discussed. Patient also interested in meeting with a chaplain/spiritual care provider.

8. GENETIC COUNSELING: Germline expanded panel pending (BRCA1/2 confirmed wildtype; ATM VUS identified; PALB2 and others pending). Genetic counseling follow-up appointment scheduled. This result will not alter immediate treatment plan but may affect surgical planning discussions (e.g., risk-reducing procedures).

9. RESTAGING AFTER NEOADJUVANT THERAPY: Bilateral breast MRI will be obtained after completion of all neoadjuvant chemotherapy and prior to surgery to assess radiographic response and guide surgical planning.

10. NEXT STEPS:
    - Patient consented to neoadjuvant Keytruda/chemotherapy today. Signed informed consent for systemic therapy and for Keytruda (immunotherapy-specific consent obtained).
    - Chemotherapy order entry initiated. Port placement (subcutaneous implantable venous access device, IVAD) referral placed for interventional radiology — scheduled for this week.
    - Cycle 1 of Keytruda + dose-dense AC scheduled to begin within 5–7 days, pending port placement and pharmacy verification.
    - Patient given oncology triage line number and instructed to call for any fevers, chills, severe nausea/vomiting, or concerning new symptoms.
    - Follow-up appointment scheduled in 2 weeks for Cycle 2 and toxicity assessment.

Time spent with patient and family: approximately 75 minutes. Extensive counseling and education provided. Patient is eager to proceed. She is well-informed, emotionally engaged, and understands the seriousness of her diagnosis as well as the goals and potential benefits of this treatment approach.

Dictated and signed by: Breast Medical Oncology Attending
